title: Fitting solid wood floorboards
subtitle: Secret-nail tongue-and-groove boards to a timber subfloor
excerpt: How to lay solid tongue-and-groove hardwood floorboards on a timber subfloor: acclimatising the timber, setting the expansion gap, cutting and fitting the boards, secret-nailing through the tongue, and fitting the final board.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. BS 8201 Code of practice for the installation of flooring (British Standards, publicly referenced). Machined-timber industry guidance.

content.text: Solid tongue-and-groove hardwood boards are fixed to a timber subfloor by
secret nailing
through the tongue. Unlike floating floors, solid boards are fixed to the subfloor at every board and at every joist crossing. The boards move seasonally as the wood responds to humidity; the
expansion gap
around the perimeter absorbs that movement. A properly fitted solid floor can be sanded and re-finished multiple times over several decades.
Step 1, acclimatise the timber

Stack the boards flat in the room where they will be laid for at least one week before fitting. Leave the packs open or separate the boards to allow air circulation. The wood must reach the same moisture content as the room before it is fixed; boards laid before acclimatisation will move after fixing, causing gaps or buckling.
Preparing the subfloor
Step 1, check the subfloor.

The existing floorboards or plywood subfloor must be flat, firm, and dry. A moisture meter reading above 12 percent indicates excessive moisture; fit a polythene vapour barrier before proceeding. Punch down any raised nails; replace any soft or punky boards.
Setting out and fitting
Step 2, set the first board.

Lay the first board with the groove side against the starting wall and leave a 10 to 15 mm expansion gap between the board and the wall. Use spacer blocks cut from off-cuts to maintain the gap while fixing. The first board is face-nailed through the face, as the groove side has no tongue to nail through. The skirting board will cover the fixing.
Step 3, secret-nail the boards.

From the second board onward, drive nails through the tongue at 45 degrees at every joist crossing. Use a nail punch to set the head below the tongue surface so the groove of the next board drops over without binding. Pull the tongue edge of each board tight against the previous board before nailing by driving a bolster chisel into the subfloor against the board edge as a lever.
Step 4, cut and close the final board.

The last board almost always needs ripping (cutting along the length) to fit the gap. Measure the width needed at each end of the last run, as the room may not be square. Cut with a circular saw or jigsaw. Face-nail through the face of the final board close to the wall; the skirting board will cover the fixing.
Finishing

Newly fitted boards can be sanded and finished immediately, or left for several weeks to allow any residual movement to settle before finishing. A drum sander and edge sander hire covers the sanding work; the floor requires three to four passes at progressively finer grits (40, 80, 120) before finishing. Apply a floor oil or hard-wax oil per the manufacturer's timing between coats.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Gaps that appear and close seasonally are normal in solid wood. A fixed gap indicates the boards were too dry when fitted; the floor needs lifting and re-laying.
attrs.cause: Boards were fitted at low humidity and have since dried further, or the boards were not acclimatised before fitting.
attrs.symptom: Gaps appearing between boards after fitting

attrs.fix: Check the subfloor moisture content. Fit a vapour barrier. Allow time for the boards to equalize; in some cases the cupping reverses naturally once the moisture balance is restored.
attrs.cause: Moisture imbalance between the top and underside of the board. High moisture content under the board is common when a vapour barrier is missing.
attrs.symptom: Boards cupping (edges higher than the centre)

attrs.fix: Powdering the joints with talc reduces friction temporarily. For a permanent fix, the creak location needs to be identified and the board re-fixed with a screw from above, set in a countersunk hole, filled and touched in.
attrs.cause: The boards are rubbing against each other at the tongue-and-groove joint, or rubbing against a joist.
